---
title: "From Mouth to Mind"
subtitle: "Data Science and Text Analysis, CAMS, CityU"
    
# date: "2023-06-12"
# date-format: "iso"

author: "Yue Hu"
institute: 
  - Department of Political Science
  - Tsinghua University

bibliography: pre_cams.bib

execute:
  echo: true
  message: false
  warning: false
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console

format:
  revealjs:
    number-sections: false
    css: https://www.drhuyue.site/slides_gh/css/style_basic.css
    theme: ../../../css/goldenBlack.scss
    # logo: https://gitlab.com/sammo3182/backup/raw/85b3c1ad4b459d7a9f901f124b936428eda5fcaf/logo_zzxx.png?inline=true
    slide-number: true
    incremental: true
    preview-links: true # open an iframe for a link
    link-external-newwindow: true
    embed-resources: false
    chalkboard: true # allwoing chalk board B, notes canvas C
    # callout-icon: false
    callout-appearance: simple
    show-slide-number: all # `speaker` only print in pdf, `all` shows all the time
    showNotes: false # 'true' or 'separate-page' if you want notes on a separate page
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-image: https://gitlab.com/sammo3182/backup/raw/85b3c1ad4b459d7a9f901f124b936428eda5fcaf/logo_THPS.png?inline=true
      data-background-size: 250px   
      data-background-position: top 10% right 5%

revealjs-plugins:
  - spotlight
  
lightbox:
  match: auto
  effect: fade

spotlight:
  size: 50
  presentingCursor: default
  toggleSpotlightOnMouseDown: false
  spotlightOnKeyPressAndHold: 73 # keycode for "i"
---

```{r setup}
#| include = FALSE

chooseCRANmirror(graphics = FALSE, ind = 1)

library(pacman)

p_load(
  icons,
  tidyverse
)

# Functions preload
set.seed(313)
```

## Who am I

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

*Experience*

- PhD. of Poli. Sci., U of Iowa
  - Grad. Certificate in Informatics
- Deputy director of Computational Social Science Lab, THU
- Founder of *Learning R with Dr. Hu and His Friends*
- Creator of 100k-download packages

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

*Method specialty*: 

- W. data science
  - Lab and survey experiments
  - Latent variable, network, spatial
  - *Text analysis, data visualization*

:::

::::

## Research Areas

- W. psychology
  - Political [cognition]{.red} (attitudes, behaviors, memory)
  - Political communication (state media, youth)
  - Identity politics (urban/rural, Hong Kong, Judiciary)
- W. economics
  - Macro economy &rarr; micro cognition &rarr; meso-policy
  - Economic [inequality]{.red}
- W. linguistics
  - Language planning and policies
  - How the language you speak [shapes]{.red} the mind of politics

## Warm-up questions {background="#43464B"}

1. Why did you get up early today? 😝
1. Without understanding what a person says, how can you tell s/he is a leader and how much power does s/he have?
1. "King" &minus; "man" + "woman" &approx; "Queen"? Can word meaning really do arithmetic?
1. (Sharper) Do you still need to learn natural language processing in the age of large language model (LLM)?
1. (Even sharper) Do you still need to learn programming in the age of LLM?


::: {.fragment .r-fit-text}

*Let's learn how to figure them out!*

:::


## Plan of the course

A three-part journey: **talk to the computer** &rarr; **teach the computer to read** &rarr; **teach the computer to "understand"**

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

*[Programming]{.red} Basic*

1. R Basics
1. Data visualization

*Text-As-Data [Preparation]{.red}*

1. Theory
1. Data scraping
    - Regular expression*
    - Text Preprocessing

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
*Text Analysis in [Practice]{.red}*

1. Word frequency analysis
1. Classification
1. Clustering
1. [Beyond "BOW"]{.gray}*

:::

::::

## Prerequisite

- You'll learn the course a little more easily, if you've known
    - A little bit econometrics
    - A little bit programming
    - But you don't have to

:::{.fragment style="text-align:right; margin-top: 1em"}

- Operation environment
  - [R](https://cran.r-project.org/mirrors.html) (why not Python?😋)
  - [`drhur`](https://sammo3182.github.io/software/drhur/)
  - [`rvest`](https://rvest.tidyverse.org/)
  - [`quanteda`](http://quanteda.io/)

:::


# Why Taking the Course [Now]{.red}

## "Can't I just ask ChatGPT?"

> LLMs already write code and read texts for me. Why should I, a liberal arts student, still learn programming and natural language processing?

:::{.fragment style="text-align:center; margin-top: 2em"}

The answer depends on your answers to [two questions]{.red}.

:::

:::{.notes}
This is the core message of the whole lecture: remember warm-up questions 4 & 5.
:::

## Q1: To *get by*, or to *get strong*?

- Asking AI for code can complete a basic task — enough to [get by]{.red}.
- But LLM-generated code (from ChatGPT & DeepSeek to Copilot) is still often
  - Incomplete or buggy;
  - Written in outdated syntax rather than today's best solution.
- Judging, fixing, and improving that code requires exactly what it was supposed to replace: [your own programming ability]{.red}.

## Q2: To *use* AI, or to *be used* by AI?

- Over half of college students already use generative AI regularly [@YanYiQiao2025] — but in two very different ways:
  - [Active]{.red} users own the knowledge and can evaluate what the model returns; for them AI is an *assistant*.
  - [Passive]{.red} users merely relay answers from the chat box to the code box; for them AI is the *teacher* — no different from believing whatever the early internet said.
- Knowing how programs run and how machines "read" text is what puts you on the active side: [shaping]{.red} the digital world instead of merely accepting it.

:::{.fragment .r-fit-text}

*The one who uses AI, or the one used by AI — which will you be?*

:::

## "But I'm a liberal arts student..."

Three misconceptions (based on interviews with students at universities across the country):

- ["Programming = math."]{.red}
  - Most data work needs only basic math — the computer does the calculating;
  - The core is *logic*: decomposing a complex problem into clear, ordered steps — a liberal arts [strength]{.red}.
- ["What can I even do with it?"]{.red}
  - Without a goal, most learners quit after a few commands;
  - This course anchors every technique to [real research questions]{.red}.
- ["Recite V-E-C-T-O-R, vector..."]{.red}
  - A language is never mastered by memorization but by [use]{.red} — so we practice from day one.

## Three advantages you already have

- A pure liberal arts student can go far
    - I majored in political science *because* it seemed math-free; now computational political science is my main field.

:::{.fragment}

1. [Language sense]{.red}: a programming language is an *artificial* language — far simpler than any natural language you have mastered; modern R reads almost like plain English.
2. [Imagination]{.red}: reframe coding from mechanical operation to *conversation* (next slide).
3. [Problem-driven learning]{.red}: you don't need to become an engineer — keep asking "*what can this do for my research?*" and you'll learn faster with less.

:::

## Programming as a conversation

- Not typing commands at a machine, but talking with ["little R"]{.red}: a three-year-old genius
  - Computes anything, draws beautifully — but speaks only English, in short sentences;
  - Understands you only when [every logical step]{.red} is spelled out.
- A "3-yr-old genius" theory
  - ![Errors are not failures --- just a child saying "I don't understand yet."](https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/figure/drhur_textbook/drhur_littleR.png){fig-align="center" height=300}

:::{.notes}

Psychological barriers, not information, are the biggest obstacle for liberal arts students. STEM students face the same frustrations but cannot quit because programming is a hard requirement for them. The conversation metaphor builds the persistence that liberal arts students otherwise lack.

:::

# Why R

## What's R

- A high-level programming language (object-oriented programming)

:::{.fragment}
![](https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/figure/languageLevel.jpg){fig-align="center" height=500}
:::


:::{.notes}

* Implement of S(tatistical) language, S3, S4 standards.

* Named after Ross Ihaka /i:haka/, and Robert Gentleman
    + Python: British comedy group Monty Python, whom Python creator Guido van Rossum enjoyed
    + STATA: Stats + Data
    + SPSS (Statistical Package for the Social Sciences, created by Norman Nie and several poli sci grads in Stanford)

* High-level > assembly language (only using ADD, MOVE, etc.) > machine language (010101) > hardware

* High-level: C, JAVA, Python, Fortran, R

* Friendly tips: STATA (C), SPSS (JAVA), SAS, Minitab

* **Object-oriented** programming (OOP): Class &rarr; objects
    + Carb &rarr; Steam ban
    + University &rarr; Tsinghua


C++, Python, Ruby, Perl, Matlab
:::



## What can R do

:::{.fragment style="text-align:center; margin-top: 1em"}
  Statistics and econometrics.
:::

:::{.fragment style="text-align:center; margin-top: 1em"}
  Crunching almost any sizes of data.
:::

:::{.fragment style="text-align:center; margin-top: 1em"}
Editing and running code from Excel, STATA, Python, C++, ...
:::

:::{.fragment style="text-align:center; margin-top: 1em"}
Scrape data from local files, websites, databases, pdf...
:::

:::{.fragment style="text-align:center; margin-top: 1em"}
Create webpages
:::

:::{.fragment style="text-align:center; margin-top: 1em"}
Write academic articles, books, or dissertations in html, pdf, or docx
:::

:::{.fragment style="text-align:center; margin-top: 1em"}
Create presentation slides
:::

:::{.fragment style="text-align:center; margin-top: 1em"}
Sending emails    
......
:::

## What's R good at

:::: {.columns style="text-align:center"}

::: {.column width="30%"}
### Analysis

- `r nrow(available.packages())`+ packages
- E.g., [Survival Task View](https://cran.r-project.org/web/views/Survival.html)
:::

::: {.column width="70%"}
### Visualization
:::{.r-stack}

![](https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/figure/ggmap.png){.fragment height=500}

![](https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/figure/hillShade.png){.fragment height=500}

![](https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/figure/distributionHill.png){.fragment height=500}

:::
:::

::::


## {background-image="https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/figure/programCompare.jpg" background-size="auto auto" background-opacity=0.1}

![](https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/figure/programCompare.jpg){fig-align="center" height=600}


# Set Up

## Installation

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
*Core*

![](https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/figure/Rlogo.png){fig-align="center" height=400}
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
*Integrated development environment (IDE)*

![](https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/figure/rstudio.jpg){fig-align="center" height=400}
:::

::::


## Packages

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="40%"}
![](https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/figure/apps.jpeg){fig-align="center" height=400}
:::

::: {.column width="60%"}

:::{.fragment}
*Installation*

```{r installPg}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true

install.packages("drhur")
remotes::install_github("sammo3182/drhur")
```
:::

:::{.fragment style="margin-top:1em"}
*Use*

```{r usingPg}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true

library(drhur) # or require(drhur)

drhur::drhur("play")
```
:::

:::

::::

## `drhur` 

- Software Installation

::: {.fragment}

```{r install}
#| eval: false

drhur(module, language = "en")
```

:::

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

- Programming module
    - Released version (2.0.0)
        - `algebra`
        - `play`
        - `visual_basic`

:::

::: {.column .fragment width="50%"}

- Text analysis (CAMS module)
    1. `scrap`
    1. `regex`*
    1. `preprocess`
    1. `frequency`
    1. `classify`

:::

::::

## CAMS Module

1. Download the files from the shared folder (see the link in the email)
1. Install the source package in the shared folder by the following code

:::{.fragment}

```{r rootPackage, echo = TRUE, eval = FALSE}
install.packages(<the_package_file>, type="source")
```

:::

## Reference
